text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Chemotherapy Assessment / Pre-Maintenance Therapy Planning

ATTENDING: [Attending Gynecologic Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology — Gynecologic Malignancies

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC), FIGO stage IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), who returns today for post-chemotherapy assessment following completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel adjuvant chemotherapy. She is here to discuss restaging results and initiation of PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a delightful 47-year-old Ghanaian-American woman with a well-characterized diagnosis of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0, AJCC 8th edition), diagnosed approximately 8 months ago. Her disease was characterized at the outset by extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis including bilateral ovarian involvement (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), omental caking, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and a markedly elevated CA-125 of 3,847 U/mL. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 38 and a maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at 55; germline testing confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (5382insC / c.5266dupC), a founder mutation also reported in West African populations. Somatic tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx) demonstrated somatic loss of heterozygosity at the BRCA1 locus, TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation, RB1 deep deletion, and high genomic LOH score of 31% with an HRD score of 68, confirming homologous recombination deficiency and very strong expected sensitivity to platinum-based therapy and PARP inhibition.

She underwent successful primary debulking surgery (PDS) approximately 7 months ago — an exploratory laparotomy with TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants from multiple sites including right paracolic gutter, sigmoid mesentery, left pelvic sidewall, and right hemidiaphragmatic stripping — achieving R0 (no gross residual disease). Surgical pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with STIC of the right fimbriated end, supporting fallopian tube origin, and 5 of 27 lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma.

She then initiated adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) / paclitaxel (175 mg/m2) every 21 days beginning approximately 6 weeks post-surgery. Her course was notable for:
- Cycle 2: Grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction (facial flushing, urticaria, mild chest tightness), managed with carboplatin 12-step desensitization protocol for all subsequent cycles without recurrence
- Cycles 1–3: Grade 2 alopecia (complete scalp hair loss), Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in fingertips
- After cycle 4: Grade 3 fatigue and Grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL) requiring transfusion of 2 units pRBC; Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral hands and feet, affecting instrumental ADLs), prompting paclitaxel dose reduction to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6. Duloxetine 30 mg daily initiated for neuropathic symptom management.
- Carboplatin maintained at full dose (AUC 5) throughout, using the desensitization protocol for cycles 2–6
- Cycles 5 and 6 completed at dose-reduced paclitaxel without further significant toxicity

She completed her 6th and final cycle of adjuvant chemotherapy approximately 4 weeks ago. A post-treatment CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed last week demonstrated no evidence of disease — no residual peritoneal implants, no ascites, no lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion. The previously noted right obturator lymphocele decreased from 2.1 cm at the pre-chemotherapy baseline scan to 0.8 cm, now considered resolved. This represents a complete radiographic response.

Today's visit is focused on reviewing the overall treatment response, discussing laboratory values, and finalizing a plan for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy.

Since completion of cycle 6, the patient reports the following:
- Fatigue has improved considerably; she is sleeping better and has more energy during the day
- Neuropathy has improved from Grade 2 to Grade 1; she reports mild tingling in the toes bilaterally but no longer has significant hand involvement, and there is no functional limitation at this point
- Hair is beginning to regrow; she notes fine scalp hair returning, which she finds encouraging
- Nausea essentially resolved since discontinuation of chemotherapy
- Appetite has returned; she has regained approximately 2 kg since last visit
- No fever, chills, or infections since chemotherapy completion
- No new neurologic symptoms, no vision changes, no bone pain
- Emotionally, she states she feels "cautiously hopeful" and continues to attend the gynecologic oncology support group, which she finds helpful

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Fatigue improving, ECOG performance status now 1 (recovering well). No fever, no night sweats, no new unintentional weight loss (weight recovering).
Neurologic: Bilateral distal toe tingling, Grade 1, improved from Grade 2 at last visit. No balance difficulties, no motor deficits.
Musculoskeletal: Mild myalgias with walking, improving. No joint effusions.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite normalized. No nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation. No abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. No pelvic pain.
Cardiovascular: No dyspnea, no chest pain, no palpitations. No leg swelling.
Dermatologic: Scalp hair regrowing. Nails somewhat brittle (persistent paclitaxel effect), improving.
Hematologic: No bruising, no bleeding, no petechiae noted by patient.
Psychosocial: Anxiety has improved with support group participation. Continues to process implications of BRCA1 carrier status. Reports ongoing discussions with sister and daughter about genetic testing.

---

MEDICATIONS:
- Duloxetine 30 mg orally once daily (peripheral neuropathy)
- Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed for nausea (rarely using now)
- Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed (rarely using)
- Multivitamin orally once daily
- Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously once daily (VTE prophylaxis post-surgery — patient states she completed the 28-day course as instructed)

ALLERGIES:
- Carboplatin: Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction (managed with desensitization protocol)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital signs: Temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), blood pressure 116/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Height 163 cm, weight 64 kg (BMI 24.1 kg/m2, up from 62 kg at last visit).

General: Well-appearing, alert, and oriented. In no acute distress. Fine hair regrowth visible on scalp; eyebrows and eyelashes partially regrowing.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary regions palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No accessory muscle use.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender. Midline surgical scar well-healed, no hernia. No palpable masses. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No shifting dullness or fluid wave (no ascites).
Pelvis: Post-hysterectomy/BSO. No adnexal fullness or pelvic mass appreciable on bimanual exam through vaginal cuff. Vaginal cuff intact and well-healed.
Extremities: No calf tenderness, no edema. Nail ridging noted bilaterally (chemotherapy-related), otherwise normal.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation: mild decreased vibration sense in bilateral toes; light touch intact in hands. Romberg negative. Gait steady.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

CA-125: 11 U/mL (NORMAL, reference <35 U/mL) — this is down from 187 U/mL at initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy and 3,847 U/mL at diagnosis. Complete biochemical normalization.
HE4: 48 pmol/L (NORMAL) — also fully normalized.

CBC:
- WBC: 6.4 K/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3.9 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (Grade 1 mild anemia, stable, recovering post-chemotherapy)
- MCV: 91 fL
- Platelets: 241 K/uL (NORMAL)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Chloride: 103 mEq/L
- CO2: 26 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.79 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Glucose: 88 mg/dL
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL
- AST: 22 U/L
- ALT: 19 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L
- Total protein: 7.2 g/dL

Thyroid function: TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal).
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal; cisplatin/carboplatin effect resolving).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Post-treatment restaging CT scan (chest, abdomen, pelvis with IV contrast) performed approximately 4 weeks after completion of cycle 6: No evidence of disease recurrence or residual malignancy. No peritoneal implants, no omental nodularity, no ascites, no pelvic masses, no lymphadenopathy. The previously noted right obturator lymphocele has decreased to 0.8 cm from 2.1 cm. Stable post-surgical changes in the pelvis. No pleural effusion, no pulmonary nodules. In summary: clean scan, nothing of concern — this is a complete radiographic response. Very gratifying result following R0 surgery and 6 cycles of chemotherapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), who has achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response following primary debulking surgery (R0 resection) and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy. CA-125 is now normalized at 11 U/mL, HE4 is normalized, and her post-treatment CT is entirely clean.

1. COMPLETE RESPONSE CONFIRMED:
She has achieved what we most hope for in this disease: R0 surgical debulking, CA-125 normalization, and no radiographic evidence of disease. Given her germline BRCA1 mutation with somatic LOH, high HRD score (68), and this complete response, she is in the best possible position for PARP inhibitor maintenance.

2. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE THERAPY:
We discussed at length the strong evidence supporting PARP inhibitor maintenance in this setting. The SOLO-1 trial demonstrated that olaparib (Lynparza) maintenance for 2 years in women with germline BRCA1/2-mutated advanced ovarian cancer who achieved a complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy resulted in a dramatic improvement in progression-free survival — 3-year PFS rate of 60% vs. 27% with placebo, with updated data showing sustained long-term benefit. The PRIMA trial similarly demonstrated benefit for niraparib (Zejula) across the broader HRD population. Given Ms. Osei-Bonsu's germline BRCA1 status and complete response, she has the strongest possible indication for maintenance.

We discussed olaparib (Lynparza 300 mg twice daily) vs. niraparib (Zejula). The patient asked about the dosing schedules; I explained that olaparib is two 150 mg tablets twice daily, whereas niraparib is a once-daily dosing regimen. We discussed potential side effects of each — for niraparib: nausea, fatigue, anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, and hypertension are the main concerns; for olaparib: nausea, fatigue, anemia, and a small but real risk of secondary myeloid malignancy with prolonged use. After discussion, the patient expressed a preference for once-daily dosing for convenience. We will proceed with niraparib (Zejula).

Dosing: Per updated PRIMA individualized dosing guidance and the prescribing information, patients with body weight less than 77 kg may start at niraparib 200 mg once daily to reduce the risk of early hematologic toxicity. Ms. Osei-Bonsu weighs 64 kg; baseline platelets today are 241 K/uL. Her platelets have fully recovered to normal post-chemotherapy. Given weight <77 kg, I will start her at niraparib 200 mg orally once daily taken at bedtime to reduce nausea.

Monitoring plan: CBC weekly for the first 8 weeks, then monthly. Blood pressure monitoring at each visit given reported hypertensive potential. CA-125 every 3 months. Next CT at 12 months from completion of chemotherapy per surveillance protocol.

Plan to continue for a total of 3 years from initiation per SOLO-1 data (though individualized based on tolerability and response).

Patient counseled in detail on:
- Nausea (most common; take at bedtime, use ondansetron 8 mg PRN, prochlorperazine as backup)
- Anemia and thrombocytopenia (dose-limiting; must monitor CBC closely; to present to infusion center or ED for significant bleeding)
- Neutropenia (less common; to call if fever >38°C)
- Hypertension (common; will monitor BP)
- Fatigue (may overlap with post-chemotherapy fatigue; expected to improve over time)
- MDS/AML risk (rare, <1% long-term; benefit clearly outweighs risk at this stage)
- No grapefruit or grapefruit juice (mild CYP interactions)
- Pregnancy: not applicable given surgical menopause from BSO, but menopause discussed; referred to gynecology for menopausal symptom management as needed

3. NEUROPATHY:
Grade 1 bilateral distal toe tingling, improved from Grade 2 at last visit. Duloxetine 30 mg daily continued. Expected to continue improving now that chemotherapy is complete. If neuropathy worsens on niraparib (not a typical side effect), we will reassess.

4. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING:
Post-treatment surveillance will include:
- CA-125 and clinical exam every 3 months for first 2 years, then every 6 months
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at 12 months from chemotherapy completion (approximately 8 months from today)
- Annual breast MRI and mammography per BRCA1 surveillance recommendations (discussed with patient; she will coordinate with her primary care and breast surgery team)
- Referral to gynecology for post-BSO menopausal symptoms management (patient has been experiencing some hot flashes; systemic HRT in BRCA1 carriers after ovarian cancer is controversial but may be considered given her young age — this will be discussed with gynecology)

5. GENETIC IMPLICATIONS:
Patient's daughter (age 22) and sister (age 44) have been referred for genetic counseling for BRCA1 testing. Patient reports her daughter is planning to proceed with testing. We discussed the importance of cascade testing for first-degree relatives.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Patient continues to attend the gynecologic oncology support group, which she finds very beneficial. Mood markedly improved compared to early treatment visits. She expresses appropriate cautious optimism. She is looking forward to returning to full-time teaching in the coming weeks as fatigue improves. No evidence of active depression requiring formal psychiatric referral at this time.

7. RETURN TO CARE:
Patient will return for CBC check in 1 week after starting niraparib, then weekly CBCs for the first 8 weeks. Next oncology follow-up in 4 weeks. She has been given a nursing triage line number to call for fever, bleeding, shortness of breath, or other concerning symptoms. Written patient education materials regarding niraparib provided.

Total visit time: 65 minutes. Majority of time spent in discussion of maintenance therapy options, expected side effects, and surveillance planning. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with the treatment plan. Questions answered to satisfaction.

[Electronically signed by Attending Gynecologic Medical Oncologist]
